body_txt: A character holds back anger until it can no longer be held back, and then the rage comes pouring through. Sometimes characters have every reason to be angry, but they try to hold it back for various reasons. Sometimes, the reasons are selfish; sometimes, they are because they know it would be wrong to get angry or that blowing their top would have bad consequences (such as losing their job, getting arrested, or even getting themselves or others killed); and sometimes, the reason is just for the sake of appearances. Eventually, there will be a last straw. It could range from a minor thing to out-and-out hitting the character's Berserk Button . But now, the flood of anger comes out at full force, erupting like a volcano, or bursting out like water from a breached dam. It usually involves Unstoppable Rage but in some cases, it can manifest as Tranquil Fury . Some stories might even have this trigger a Heroic Second Wind . In some cases, the characters may devolve into Angrish as they lose the ability to speak or even think coherently due to the sheer rage they're experiencing, with particularly severe cases graduating from rage-filled seething to blood-curdling screams of pure, unbridled fury . Also, if the character's Berserk Button is hit, this character might even be twice as berserk as usual. Villains as well can hit this rage breaking point, typically when their schemes have become unraveled or they are losing to the hero and begin suffering a Villainous Breakdown . In such situations, they may begin Hulking Out into their One-Winged Angel form, or simply lose all calm and begin irrationally rushing the hero . It's possible the villain has shown themselves to be Affably Evil or a façade thereof , and they break as they become Ax-Crazy . Suddenly Shouting often goes hand-in-hand with this, and the character might fly into a Reason You Suck rant. This response can occur after the character has Stopped Dead in Their Tracks in an attempt to control their anger. A Sister Trope to Trying Not to Cry (a tears breaking point), Beware the Nice Ones (characters who often try not to get angry) and Chewing the Scenery . Compare: Contrast Hair-Trigger Temper (who never bothers trying to hold back), The Stoic (who almost never gets pissed in the first place) and Passive-Aggressive Kombat (a way to avoid open fights, while still being hostile).
